title: Lévy-like flights and fractal geometry of finite point sets
authors: Konstantinos Chalas, F. K. Diakonos, A. S. Kapoyannis
abstract: We study Lévy-like and truncated Lévy-like flights with step probability distribution of the form $r^{-1+ν}$ for negative, positive, and zero $ν$, focusing on the appearance of fractal geometry characteristics in the generated point sets. Forming ensembles of such point sets with fixed multiplicity, we develop simulation techniques leading to the desired value of correlation dimension in a vast continuous interval of scales. In particular, we demonstrate the possibility to produce ensembles of data sets with a low number of points with the needed properties. Furthermore, we show that the positive $ν$ distributions, apart from a region near the upper scale limit, show fractal behaviour that extends to infinitesimally low scales. As an example, we apply our findings to producing simulations relevant to the search for critical fluctuations, related to QCD critical endpoint, in heavy-ion collision experiments.
summary: This paper provides a mathematical framework for generating and analyzing finite point sets with specific fractal dimensions using generalized Lévy flights. It demonstrates how to control scaling regions and suggests that low-multiplicity datasets can effectively represent fractal properties, with applications to simulating critical fluctuations in heavy-ion physics.
main_problem: Developing a unified framework to analyze the scaling behavior and fractal geometry of finite point sets generated by Lévy-like and truncated Lévy-like flights, specifically controlling the range of the scaling region.
main_result: The study proves that the correlation integral distribution is independent of the number of points in a set and demonstrates that for positive nu, fractal behavior can extend to infinitesimally low scales, making it ideal for low-multiplicity simulations.
method: Numerical simulations of Lévy-like walks using Markovian procedures, application of the correlation integral C(R) and ring techniques, and mathematical proofs for subsampling and ensemble generation.
model_or_system: Lévy-like and truncated Lévy-like flights characterized by a step probability distribution P(r) proportional to r^(-1+nu), implemented in 1D, 2D, and 3D embedding spaces.
key_observables: Correlation integral C(R), fractal correlation dimension dF, and local log-log slopes dF.
important_parameters: Step exponent nu, multiplicity Nm, embedding dimension d, and step limits a (upper) and b (lower).
main_assumptions: The analysis of Poisson-distributed multiplicities assumes the exclusion of events with zero or one track; the asymptotic form assumes all tracks share the same upper limit a.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares C(R) distributions for different subsets of a Lévy walk; Figure 3 shows C(R) and local slopes for positive nu; Figure 6 illustrates the impact of limits a and b on local slopes; Figure 12 shows the divergence of slopes for different dimensions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Lévy-like flight model, develops mathematical techniques for analyzing finite point sets (subsampling and ensemble generation), investigates the effect of step limits (a and b) on scaling, and concludes with an application to simulating QCD critical fluctuations in heavy-ion collisions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The C(R) distribution of an arbitrary point set (a L´evy walk with step probability exponent ν = −1/3 for the ν −ϕ case, embedding space dimension d =2, lower step limit b and no upper step limit). With continuous line we depict the C(R) of all the points of the set which is Nm = 103. The various symbols correspond to the C(R) curves for sets of different number of points drawn randomly from the full set consisting of Nm steps. In the caption Nm = j/k denotes (here and in the rest of the paper) j points taken from a full set of k points. The increased fluctuations for Nm = 2 and number of events Ne = 104

Figure 2: L´evy walks with varying multiplicities Nm for fixed step probability exponent ν = −1/3 < 0. The walks are carried out in d =1,2 and 3 embedding spaces. We show the ν−ϕ and ν−ν cases (coinciding for d = 1). No upper limit a is imposed in the steps. Lower limit bef is the same in ν −ϕ and ν −ν cases. With thin lines we present the straight lines which are approached by the distributions in each case (shrinked to a point for Nm = 2). (i) The correlation integral C of each set as function of the scale R/bef in log-log plot. (ii) The local slopes dF of the sets presented in (i).

Figure 3: L´evy walks with varying multiplicities Nm for fixed step probability exponent ν = +1/3 > 0. The walks are carried out in d =1,2 and 3 embedding spaces. We show the ν−ϕ and ν−ν cases (coinciding for d = 1). No lower limit b is imposed in the steps. Upper limit is a in all cases. With thin lines we present the straight lines which are approached by the distributions in each case. In the ν′ −ν′ cases, ν′ = ν/d. (i) The correlation integral C of each set as function of the scale R/a in log-log plot. (ii) The local slopes dF of the sets presented in (i).

Figure 5: L´evy walks with varying step probability exponents ν > 0 for fixed multiplicity Nm = 10 and number of events Nm = 106. The walks are carried out in d =1,2 and 3 dimensions of embedding spaces. We show the ν −ϕ and ν −ν cases (coinciding for d = 1). No lower limit b is imposed in the steps. Higher limit is a in ν −ϕ and ν −ν cases. With thin lines we present the straight lines which are approached by the distributions in each case. (i) The correlation integral C of each set as function of the scale R/a in log-log plot. (ii) The local slopes dF of the sets presented in (i).

Figure 6: L´evy walks with step probability exponent ν = −1/3 and varying upper step limits a for ν −ϕ case. In (A) multiplicity Nm = 103 and number of events Ne = 104, while in (B) Nm = 35 and Ne = 105. (i) The correlation integral C of each set as function of the scale R/b in log-log plot. (ii) The local slopes dF = d(lnC)/d(lnR) of the walks presented in (i). (iii) The derivatives R·d′ F = R·d(dF )/dR of the slopes presented in (ii). With long arrows optimal linear parts in log-log plot of C(R), dF1 and with short arrows local minimum slopes, dF2, are denoted.
